summary: idlidosa: TLS-validation disable + Microsoft Edge WebView2 process masquerade with scheduled-task persistence + silent relay of user content through author-owned Groq keys
details:
On startup, the package disables TLS certificate validation globally for the Node process by setting `process.env.NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED = "0"` at top level of dist/shared/index.js. Every outbound HTTPS request from the running daemon — not just calls to api.groq.com — becomes interceptable by any network-position attacker, removing TLS guarantees from the installer's machine for the lifetime of the process.

On Windows, dist/cli/index.js's `disguiseElectron()` copies the bundled electron.exe to `msedgewebview2.exe` and uses a shipped `rcedit` binary to forge the file's version resources (CompanyName="Microsoft Corporation", FileDescription="Microsoft Edge WebView2", OriginalFilename="msedgewebview2.exe"). The daemon and guard processes additionally set `process.title = "Windows Audio Device Graph Isolation"`. An administrator inspecting Task Manager sees what appears to be legitimate Microsoft system binaries.

`installResurrector()` then drops `%APPDATA%/Idlidosa/resurrect.js` and registers a Windows Scheduled Task named `MicrosoftEdgeWebView2Update` running every minute via `schtasks /create... /sc MINUTE /mo 1`, which respawns the disguised electron + daemon after kill or reboot. The guard script's own comment (`// fast enough to beat TestPad's 30s scan`) documents that the persistence cadence is tuned to evade detection by proctoring software.

In addition, dist/shared/index.js decrypts a bundled AES-256-GCM key pool (shared/keys.json, decryption passphrase `pageai-pool-v2` hardcoded as a decimal-byte array) and, by default, routes every screenshot OCR prompt and user question through the author's Groq accounts — so user-supplied screen contents are silently relayed through author-controlled API keys with no opt-out on first install.

The combined behaviors — globally disabled TLS, Microsoft-impersonating binary on disk, minute-cadence scheduled-task persistence under a Microsoft-branded task name, and silent relay of user content through author-owned third-party API accounts — are hostile to the installer's machine and to anyone observing it, well beyond the advertised overlay functionality.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
